# Business Glossary - Advanced Healthcare Management System ## Patient Management **Patient** An individual receiving medical care, treatment, or services from the healthcare organization. Each patient has a unique medical record number and may have multiple episodes of care across different time periods. Patients can be identified by their medical record number (MRN), which is distinct from any external insurance identifiers. **Patient Registration** The process of capturing essential demographic and administrative information when a patient first presents to the healthcare system. This includes personal details, emergency contacts, insurance information, and consent forms. ## Clinical Concepts **Diagnosis** The identification of a disease, condition, or injury based on clinical evaluation, laboratory results, and diagnostic imaging. Diagnoses are coded using ICD-10-CM (International Classification of Diseases, 10th Revision, Clinical Modification) for billing and epidemiological purposes. A patient may have multiple concurrent diagnoses with varying levels of clinical priority. **Principal Diagnosis** The primary condition identified after study as chiefly responsible for admission of the patient to the healthcare facility. This diagnosis determines the primary diagnosis-related group (DRG) for billing purposes. **Comorbidity** Any additional diagnosis that exists simultaneously with the principal diagnosis. Comorbidities affect patient complexity scores and reimbursement levels under risk-adjusted payment models. **Treatment** Any medical intervention, therapeutic procedure, or clinical action taken to address a diagnosed condition. Treatments range from medication administration to surgical procedures and behavioral therapies. Each treatment is linked to specific diagnoses through clinical justification. **Medication** Any pharmaceutical substance administered to a patient for therapeutic purposes. Medications include prescription drugs, over-the-counter medications, and controlled substances. Each medication record includes dosage, route of administration, frequency, and prescribing provider. **Medication Reconciliation** The process of comparing a patient's medication orders to all medications the patient has been taking to avoid errors, omissions, duplications, and drug interactions. **LabResult** The outcome of diagnostic laboratory tests performed on patient specimens (blood, urine, tissue, etc.). Results include quantitative values, reference ranges, abnormality flags, and interpreting pathologist notes. Results are timestamped and linked to specific test orders. ## Provider & Organizational Structure **Provider** A licensed healthcare professional authorized to deliver medical services within their scope of practice. Provider types include physicians (MD/DO), nurse practitioners (NP), physician assistants (PA), registered nurses (RN), and clinical specialists. Each provider has unique National Provider Identifier (NPI) numbers and hospital credentialing status. **Attending Physician** The primary physician responsible for a patient's care during an admission or encounter. This physician has ultimate authority over treatment decisions and discharge planning. **Consulting Physician** A specialist called upon to provide expert opinion or manage specific aspects of a patient's condition beyond the attending physician's expertise. **Department** An organizational unit within the healthcare facility dedicated to specific medical specialties or services (e.g., Emergency Department, Cardiology, Oncology, Radiology). Departments have hierarchical relationships and may be subdivided into sub-specialties. **Service Line** A strategic grouping of related departments focused on a specific patient population or disease continuum (e.g., Heart & Vascular, Neuroscience, Orthopedics). Service lines are used for operational and financial reporting.

## Financial & Administrative **Insurance** A third-party payer contract that provides coverage for medical services. Insurance types include private commercial plans, Medicare (federal), Medicaid (state), and TRICARE (military). Each insurance plan has specific coverage policies, prior authorization requirements, and cost-sharing structures. **Prior Authorization** The process of obtaining advance approval from an insurance payer before certain services, treatments, or medications can be provided. Authorization requirements vary by payer, procedure, and medical necessity criteria. **Claim** A formal request for payment submitted to an insurance payer for services rendered to an insured patient. Claims include procedure codes (CPT/HCPCS), diagnosis codes (ICD-10), modifiers, and charges. Claims may be submitted on a fee-for-service or bundled payment basis. **Explanation of Benefits (EOB)** A document from the insurance payer explaining what services were covered, what portion was paid to the provider, and what financial responsibility remains for the patient (deductibles, copayments, coinsurance). **Coordination of Benefits (COB)** The process of determining which insurance plan pays first when a patient is covered by multiple payers. Rules are governed by state law and contract terms. ## Scheduling & Access **Appointment** A scheduled encounter between a patient and a provider for clinical services. Appointments can be in-person office visits, telehealth consultations, or procedural sessions. Status types include scheduled, confirmed, checked-in, in-progress, completed, canceled, and no-show. **Appointment Type** The classification of an appointment based on purpose, duration, and resource requirements. Types include new patient evaluation, established patient follow-up, annual wellness visit, procedure, and imaging. **Patient Access** The functional area responsible for scheduling, registration, insurance verification, pre-certification, and financial counseling. Patient Access departments are often the first point of contact for patients entering the healthcare system. ## Clinical Workflows **Clinical Documentation** The creation and maintenance of electronic health record entries reflecting patient care, clinical decision-making, and treatment plans. Documentation must meet regulatory standards for completeness, accuracy, and timeliness. **Care Coordination** The deliberate organization of patient care activities between multiple participants involved in a patient's care to facilitate appropriate delivery of healthcare services. Coordination includes transitions of care, specialist referrals, and post-acute planning. **Transitions of Care** The movement of patients between healthcare settings, locations, or levels of care. Critical transition points include admission, transfer between units, discharge to post-acute facilities, and home health referral. ## Data Quality & Integrity **Data Governance** The policies, processes, and standards ensuring high-quality data throughout its lifecycle. Governance includes data stewardship, quality metrics, privacy protocols, and audit trails. **Historization** The practice of maintaining time-bounded versions of data records to capture state changes over time. Effective dating (valid_from, valid_to) allows reconstruction of historical states for auditing and clinical review. **Soft Delete** A logical deletion mechanism where records are marked as inactive rather than physically removed. Soft deletes preserve audit trails and allow recovery of accidentally deleted records. ## Regulatory & Privacy **HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act)** Federal legislation regulating the use and disclosure of Protected Health Information (PHI). HIPAA establishes national standards for privacy, security, and breach notification. **Protected Health Information (PHI)** Individually identifiable health information transmitted or maintained in electronic, paper, or oral forms. PHI includes demographic data, medical histories, test results, and insurance information. **Minimum Necessary Standard** The HIPAA principle that only the minimum amount of PHI necessary to accomplish the intended purpose should be used, disclosed, or requested. **Patient Privacy Rights** Under HIPAA, patients have rights to access their medical records, request amendments, obtain an accounting of disclosures, and request restrictions on certain uses and disclosures of their information.
per_question.expected_answer: Join patients to treatments on patient_id, treatments to providers on provider_id, and providers to departments on department_id. Filter where department_name = 'Cardiology' (or department_code). Return patient MRN, name, treatment_name, treatment_date, and provider name. Exclude soft-deleted records from all tables. This query supports departmental workload analysis.

per_question.question: What treatments have been performed for a patient's specific diagnosis?
per_question.answer:
The retrieved context does not provide actual patient-level treatment records, so it cannot determine which specific treatments were performed for an individual patient’s diagnosis.

What it does show is how to identify them in the schema. The `TREATMENTS` table stores “medical treatments and procedures performed on patients” and includes `patient_id`, `diagnosis_id`, `treatment_name`, `cpt_code`, `provider_id`, `department_id`, `treatment_date`, `treatment_status`, and `notes`. In that table, `diagnosis_id` is the “primary diagnosis indication for treatment,” and `patient_id` identifies the patient receiving the treatment.

The related diagnosis details are in `DIAGNOSES`, which includes `diagnosis_id`, `patient_id`, `icd_10_code`, `diagnosis_name`, `diagnosis_type`, `provider_id`, and `diagnosis_date`. The glossary also states that each treatment is linked to specific diagnoses through clinical justification.

So, to answer this question from the database, you would look up rows in `TREATMENTS` for the relevant `patient_id` and `diagnosis_id`, optionally joining to `DIAGNOSES` on `TREATMENTS.diagnosis_id = DIAGNOSES.diagnosis_id` and confirming the same patient via `patient_id`. The returned treatment details would come from `TREATMENTS.treatment_name`, `cpt_code`, `treatment_date`, `treatment_status`, `provider_id`, `department_id`, and `notes`.

per_question.question: Which providers have prescribed medications to patients with a specific diagnosis?
per_question.answer:
The retrieved context does not contain patient-level medication or diagnosis records, so I cannot name the actual providers who have prescribed medications to patients with a specific diagnosis. It contains schema and business metadata only.

What can be answered from the context is how to identify them in the schema. The relevant tables are DIAGNOSES, MEDICATIONS, and PROVIDERS.

DIAGNOSES stores patient diagnosis records with ICD-10 coding and temporal tracking. Its key columns include diagnosis_id, patient_id, icd_10_code, diagnosis_name, diagnosis_type, provider_id, diagnosis_date, and resolution_date. The glossary also states that diagnoses are coded using ICD-10-CM, and diagnosis_type can classify diagnoses such as principal, comorbidity, admitting, or secondary.

MEDICATIONS stores patient medication prescriptions and administrations. From the retrieved schema and glossary, medication records include patient_id, medication_name, ndc_code, dosage, route, frequency, and a prescribing provider attribute. The schema excerpt is truncated after a column beginning with prescribing_prov, so the full physical column name and its exact constraint are not fully visible in the provided context, but the glossary explicitly says each medication record includes the prescribing provider.

PROVIDERS stores healthcare provider information and credentials. Its columns include provider_id, npi, first_name, last_name, provider_type, specialty, department_id, and is_active.

So, at schema level, the way to answer the question is:
Use DIAGNOSES to find the patients with the specific diagnosis, typically by filtering DIAGNOSES.icd_10_code or DIAGNOSES.diagnosis_name. Then join those diagnosis-bearing patients to MEDICATIONS through patient_id, because both tables contain patient_id. From the medication records, use the prescribing provider field in MEDICATIONS to connect to PROVIDERS.provider_id and return the provider’s identifying attributes such as first_name, last_name, npi, provider_type, and specialty.

One important limitation from the context is that TREATMENTS has an explicit diagnosis_id foreign key to DIAGNOSES, but no such diagnosis_id link is shown for MEDICATIONS in the retrieved excerpt. That means the schema evidence provided supports finding providers who prescribed medications to patients who have a given diagnosis, via shared patient_id, but it does not prove from the retrieved context alone that a specific medication is directly tied to a specific diagnosis record. The context also does not provide any actual rows, so it cannot tell which providers those are in practice.

This diagnosis determines the primary diagnosis-related group (DRG) for billing purposes. **Comorbidity** Any additional diagnosis that exists simultaneously with the principal diagnosis. Comorbidities affect patient complexity scores and reimbursement levels under risk-adjusted payment models. **Treatment** Any medical intervention, therapeutic procedure, or clinical action taken to address a diagnosed condition. Treatments range from medication administration to surgical procedures and behavioral therapies. Each treatment is linked to specific diagnoses through clinical justification. **Medication** Any pharmaceutical substance administered to a patient for therapeutic purposes. Medications include prescription drugs, over-the-counter medications, and controlled substances. Each medication record includes dosage, route of administration, frequency, and prescribing provider. **Medication Reconciliation** The process of comparing a patient's medication orders to all medications the patient has been taking to avoid errors, omissions, duplications, and drug interactions. **LabResult** The outcome of diagnostic laboratory tests performed on patient specimens (blood, urine, tissue, etc.). Results include quantitative values, reference ranges, abnormality flags, and interpreting pathologist notes. Results are timestamped and linked to specific test orders. ## Provider & Organizational Structure **Provider** A licensed healthcare professional authorized to deliver medical services within their scope of practice. Provider types include physicians (MD/DO), nurse practitioners (NP), physician assistants (PA), registered nurses (RN), and clinical specialists. Each provider has unique National Provider Identifier (NPI) numbers and hospital credentialing status. **Attending Physician** The primary physician responsible for a patient's care during an admission or encounter. This physician has ultimate authority over treatment decisions and discharge planning. **Consulting Physician** A specialist called upon to provide expert opinion or manage specific aspects of a patient's condition beyond the attending physician's expertise. **Department** An organizational unit within the healthcare facility dedicated to specific medical specialties or services (e.g., Emergency Department, Cardiology, Oncology, Radiology). Departments have hierarchical relationships and may be subdivided into sub-specialties. **Service Line** A strategic grouping of related departments focused on a specific patient population or disease continuum (e.g., Heart & Vascular, Neuroscience, Orthopedics). Service lines are used for operational and financial reporting.

## Financial & Administrative **Insurance** A third-party payer contract that provides coverage for medical services. Insurance types include private commercial plans, Medicare (federal), Medicaid (state), and TRICARE (military). Each insurance plan has specific coverage policies, prior authorization requirements, and cost-sharing structures. **Prior Authorization** The process of obtaining advance approval from an insurance payer before certain services, treatments, or medications can be provided. Authorization requirements vary by payer, procedure, and medical necessity criteria. **Claim** A formal request for payment submitted to an insurance payer for services rendered to an insured patient. Claims include procedure codes (CPT/HCPCS), diagnosis codes (ICD-10), modifiers, and charges. Claims may be submitted on a fee-for-service or bundled payment basis. **Explanation of Benefits (EOB)** A document from the insurance payer explaining what services were covered, what portion was paid to the provider, and what financial responsibility remains for the patient (deductibles, copayments, coinsurance). **Coordination of Benefits (COB)** The process of determining which insurance plan pays first when a patient is covered by multiple payers. Rules are governed by state law and contract terms. ## Scheduling & Access **Appointment** A scheduled encounter between a patient and a provider for clinical services. Appointments can be in-person office visits, telehealth consultations, or procedural sessions. Status types include scheduled, confirmed, checked-in, in-progress, completed, canceled, and no-show. **Appointment Type** The classification of an appointment based on purpose, duration, and resource requirements. Types include new patient evaluation, established patient follow-up, annual wellness visit, procedure, and imaging. **Patient Access** The functional area responsible for scheduling, registration, insurance verification, pre-certification, and financial counseling. Patient Access departments are often the first point of contact for patients entering the healthcare system. ## Clinical Workflows **Clinical Documentation** The creation and maintenance of electronic health record entries reflecting patient care, clinical decision-making, and treatment plans. Documentation must meet regulatory standards for completeness, accuracy, and timeliness. **Care Coordination** The deliberate organization of patient care activities between multiple participants involved in a patient's care to facilitate appropriate delivery of healthcare services. Coordination includes transitions of care, specialist referrals, and post-acute planning. **Transitions of Care** The movement of patients between healthcare settings, locations, or levels of care. Critical transition points include admission, transfer between units, discharge to post-acute facilities, and home health referral. ## Data Quality & Integrity **Data Governance** The policies, processes, and standards ensuring high-quality data throughout its lifecycle. Governance includes data stewardship, quality metrics, privacy protocols, and audit trails. **Historization** The practice of maintaining time-bounded versions of data records to capture state changes over time. Effective dating (valid_from, valid_to) allows reconstruction of historical states for auditing and clinical review. **Soft Delete** A logical deletion mechanism where records are marked as inactive rather than physically removed. Soft deletes preserve audit trails and allow recovery of accidentally deleted records. ## Regulatory & Privacy **HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act)** Federal legislation regulating the use and disclosure of Protected Health Information (PHI). HIPAA establishes national standards for privacy, security, and breach notification. **Protected Health Information (PHI)** Individually identifiable health information transmitted or maintained in electronic, paper, or oral forms. PHI includes demographic data, medical histories, test results, and insurance information. **Minimum Necessary Standard** The HIPAA principle that only the minimum amount of PHI necessary to accomplish the intended purpose should be used, disclosed, or requested. **Patient Privacy Rights** Under HIPAA, patients have rights to access their medical records, request amendments, obtain an accounting of disclosures, and request restrictions on certain uses and disclosures of their information.

per_question.expected_answer: Join diagnoses to patients on patient_id, patients to medications on patient_id, and medications to providers on prescribing_provider_id. Filter by specific icd_10_code. per_question.question: What is the complete medication history for a patient including prescribing providers?
per_question.answer:
The retrieved context does not provide patient-specific medication rows for a particular individual, so an actual patient’s medication history cannot be enumerated from it. Based on the table definitions, the complete medication history for a patient would be taken from MEDICATIONS and linked to the prescribing provider through PROVIDERS.

Use MEDICATIONS as the primary table, filtering the patient through MEDICATIONS.patient_id. If you need patient identification details, join MEDICATIONS.patient_id to PATIENTS.patient_id; PATIENTS includes patient_id, mrn, first_name, last_name, date_of_birth, and gender, and mrn is the unique medical record number. To include the prescribing provider, join MEDICATIONS.prescribing_provider_id to PROVIDERS.provider_id.

From MEDICATIONS, the medication history fields available are medication_id, patient_id, medication_name, ndc_code, dosage, route, frequency, prescribing_provider_id, start_date, end_date, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, and updated_at. The table purpose says it stores patient medication prescriptions and administrations, and the glossary states each medication record includes dosage, route of administration, frequency, and prescribing provider. The route field is constrained to oral, IV, IM, topical, inhaled, or subcutaneous.

From PROVIDERS, the prescribing provider details available are provider_id, npi, first_name, last_name, provider_type, specialty, department_id, and is_active. The glossary defines Provider as a licensed healthcare professional, and provider types include MD, DO, NP, PA, RN, and clinical specialists; the table constraint for provider_type includes MD, DO, NP, PA, RN, PT, and Other.

If “complete medication history” means historical as well as current state, the schema supports that through start_date and end_date for the medication period, valid_from and valid_to for historization, and is_deleted for soft delete status. So the schema-level path is: PATIENTS.patient_id → MEDICATIONS.patient_id, and MEDICATIONS.prescribing_provider_id → PROVIDERS.provider_id.
contexts: Business concept 'Each medication record' is implemented by physical table MEDICATIONS (columns: medication_id, patient_id, medication_name, ndc_code, dosage, route, frequency, prescribing_provider_id, start_date, end_date, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, updated_at).

Treatments: The table purpose explicitly states it stores medical treatments and procedures performed on patients, and the columns support that with treatment name, CPT code, date, status, provider, patient, and diagnosis references. This is a direct physical implementation of the Treatments concept rather than a related entity like diagnoses or encounters.

Business concept 'Treatments' — The table purpose explicitly states it stores medical treatments and procedures performed on patients, and the columns support that with treatment name, CPT code, date, status, provider, patient, and diagnosis references. This is a direct physical implementation of the Treatments concept rather than a related entity like diagnoses or encounters. is implemented by physical table TREATMENTS (columns: treatment_id, patient_id, diagnosis_id, treatment_name, cpt_code, provider_id, department_id, treatment_date, treatment_status, notes, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, updated_at).

# Business Glossary - Advanced Healthcare Management System ## Patient Management **Patient** An individual receiving medical care, treatment, or services from the healthcare organization. Each patient has a unique medical record number and may have multiple episodes of care across different time periods. Patients can be identified by their medical record number (MRN), which is distinct from any external insurance identifiers. **Patient Registration** The process of capturing essential demographic and administrative information when a patient first presents to the healthcare system. This includes personal details, emergency contacts, insurance information, and consent forms. ## Clinical Concepts **Diagnosis** The identification of a disease, condition, or injury based on clinical evaluation, laboratory results, and diagnostic imaging. Diagnoses are coded using ICD-10-CM (International Classification of Diseases, 10th Revision, Clinical Modification) for billing and epidemiological purposes. A patient may have multiple concurrent diagnoses with varying levels of clinical priority. **Principal Diagnosis** The primary condition identified after study as chiefly responsible for admission of the patient to the healthcare facility. This diagnosis determines the primary diagnosis-related group (DRG) for billing purposes. **Comorbidity** Any additional diagnosis that exists simultaneously with the principal diagnosis. Comorbidities affect patient complexity scores and reimbursement levels under risk-adjusted payment models. **Treatment** Any medical intervention, therapeutic procedure, or clinical action taken to address a diagnosed condition. Treatments range from medication administration to surgical procedures and behavioral therapies. Each treatment is linked to specific diagnoses through clinical justification. **Medication** Any pharmaceutical substance administered to a patient for therapeutic purposes. Medications include prescription drugs, over-the-counter medications, and controlled substances. Each medication record includes dosage, route of administration, frequency, and prescribing provider. **Medication Reconciliation** The process of comparing a patient's medication orders to all medications the patient has been taking to avoid errors, omissions, duplications, and drug interactions. **LabResult** The outcome of diagnostic laboratory tests performed on patient specimens (blood, urine, tissue, etc.). Results include quantitative values, reference ranges, abnormality flags, and interpreting pathologist notes. Results are timestamped and linked to specific test orders. ## Provider & Organizational Structure **Provider** A licensed healthcare professional authorized to deliver medical services within their scope of practice. Provider types include physicians (MD/DO), nurse practitioners (NP), physician assistants (PA), registered nurses (RN), and clinical specialists. Each provider has unique National Provider Identifier (NPI) numbers and hospital credentialing status. **Attending Physician** The primary physician responsible for a patient's care during an admission or encounter. This physician has ultimate authority over treatment decisions and discharge planning. **Consulting Physician** A specialist called upon to provide expert opinion or manage specific aspects of a patient's condition beyond the attending physician's expertise. **Department** An organizational unit within the healthcare facility dedicated to specific medical specialties or services (e.g., Emergency Department, Cardiology, Oncology, Radiology). Departments have hierarchical relationships and may be subdivided into sub-specialties. **Service Line** A strategic grouping of related departments focused on a specific patient population or disease continuum (e.g., Heart & Vascular, Neuroscience, Orthopedics). Service lines are used for operational and financial reporting.

per_question.question: Which departments have the highest volume of patient appointments?
per_question.answer:
I cannot determine which specific departments have the highest appointment volume from the retrieved context because it contains schema and business metadata only, not actual appointment records or department-level counts.

From the schema, the relevant source is the APPOINTMENTS table, which stores patient appointment scheduling and encounter information. It includes appointment_id and department_id, where department_id is a foreign key to departments.department_id. To determine highest-volume departments, you would count APPOINTMENTS.appointment_id grouped by APPOINTMENTS.department_id, and join department_id to the departments table to retrieve department names.

The context also shows that APPOINTMENTS is historized and soft-deletable: it has is_deleted, valid_from, and valid_to. Based on the glossary, is_deleted should be used to exclude logically deleted records if you want active records only, and valid_from/valid_to support point-in-time historical counts. If volume should be limited to certain workflow states, the table also has appointment_status, and the glossary defines appointment statuses as scheduled, confirmed, checked-in, in-progress, completed, canceled, and no-show. Without the underlying data, however, no actual department ranking can be identified.

# Business Glossary - Advanced Healthcare Management System ## Patient Management **Patient** An individual receiving medical care, treatment, or services from the healthcare organization. Each patient has a unique medical record number and may have multiple episodes of care across different time periods. Patients can be identified by their medical record number (MRN), which is distinct from any external insurance identifiers. **Patient Registration** The process of capturing essential demographic and administrative information when a patient first presents to the healthcare system. This includes personal details, emergency contacts, insurance information, and consent forms. ## Clinical Concepts **Diagnosis** The identification of a disease, condition, or injury based on clinical evaluation, laboratory results, and diagnostic imaging. Diagnoses are coded using ICD-10-CM (International Classification of Diseases, 10th Revision, Clinical Modification) for billing and epidemiological purposes. A patient may have multiple concurrent diagnoses with varying levels of clinical priority. **Principal Diagnosis** The primary condition identified after study as chiefly responsible for admission of the patient to the healthcare facility. This diagnosis determines the primary diagnosis-related group (DRG) for billing purposes. **Comorbidity** Any additional diagnosis that exists simultaneously with the principal diagnosis. Comorbidities affect patient complexity scores and reimbursement levels under risk-adjusted payment models. **Treatment** Any medical intervention, therapeutic procedure, or clinical action taken to address a diagnosed condition. Treatments range from medication administration to surgical procedures and behavioral therapies. Each treatment is linked to specific diagnoses through clinical justification. **Medication** Any pharmaceutical substance administered to a patient for therapeutic purposes. Medications include prescription drugs, over-the-counter medications, and controlled substances. Each medication record includes dosage, route of administration, frequency, and prescribing provider. **Medication Reconciliation** The process of comparing a patient's medication orders to all medications the patient has been taking to avoid errors, omissions, duplications, and drug interactions. **LabResult** The outcome of diagnostic laboratory tests performed on patient specimens (blood, urine, tissue, etc.). Results include quantitative values, reference ranges, abnormality flags, and interpreting pathologist notes. Results are timestamped and linked to specific test orders. ## Provider & Organizational Structure **Provider** A licensed healthcare professional authorized to deliver medical services within their scope of practice. Provider types include physicians (MD/DO), nurse practitioners (NP), physician assistants (PA), registered nurses (RN), and clinical specialists. Each provider has unique National Provider Identifier (NPI) numbers and hospital credentialing status. **Attending Physician** The primary physician responsible for a patient's care during an admission or encounter. This physician has ultimate authority over treatment decisions and discharge planning. **Consulting Physician** A specialist called upon to provide expert opinion or manage specific aspects of a patient's condition beyond the attending physician's expertise. **Department** An organizational unit within the healthcare facility dedicated to specific medical specialties or services (e.g., Emergency Department, Cardiology, Oncology, Radiology). Departments have hierarchical relationships and may be subdivided into sub-specialties. **Service Line** A strategic grouping of related departments focused on a specific patient population or disease continuum (e.g., Heart & Vascular, Neuroscience, Orthopedics). Service lines are used for operational and financial reporting.

# Data Dictionary - Advanced Healthcare Management System

## Database Overview
**Database Name**: `advanced_healthcare_db` **Schema Version**: `3.2.1` **Environment**: Production **Last Updated**: 2025-03-15 --- ## PATIENTS Table **Purpose**: Store current and historical patient demographic and administrative information. | Column Name | Data Type | Constraint | Description | |-------------|-----------|------------|-------------| | patient_id | INTEGER | PRIMARY KEY | Surrogate key for patient records | | mrn | VARCHAR(20) | UNIQUE NOT NULL | Medical Record Number - unique patient identifier | | first_name | VARCHAR(50) | NOT NULL | Patient's legal first name | | last_name | VARCHAR(50) | NOT NULL | Patient's legal last name | | date_of_birth | DATE | NOT NULL | Patient's date of birth for age calculation | | gender | VARCHAR(10) | NOT NULL, CHECK IN ('M', 'F', 'O', 'U') | Gender: Male

## Billing & Revenue Cycle **Charge Capture** The process of recording services and procedures for billing purposes. Charges must be captured at the time of service and linked to appropriate billing codes. **Claim Scrubbing** Automated validation of claims against payer-specific rules, coding guidelines, and regulatory requirements before submission. Scrubbing reduces claim rejections and denials. **Denial Management** The systematic process of tracking, analyzing, and appealing claim denials from payers. Effective denial management identifies root causes and implements corrective actions. **Revenue Cycle Management** The administrative and clinical functions that contribute to the capture, management, and collection of patient service revenue. The cycle spans from patient scheduling through final payment collection. ## Quality & Performance **Clinical Quality Measures** Standardized metrics assessing the quality of care delivered across specific conditions and procedures. Examples include readmission rates, mortality rates, complication rates, and adherence to evidence-based guidelines. **Value-Based Care** Payment models that reward healthcare providers for delivering high-quality, cost-efficient care rather than volume of services. Models include Accountable Care Organizations (ACOs) and bundled payments. **Population Health** The health outcomes of a group of individuals, including the distribution of such outcomes within the group. Population health management focuses on improving outcomes for defined patient panels through preventive care and chronic disease management.

Patient Demographics: The table stores patient identity, contact, insurance linkage, and historized administrative/demographic attributes, which aligns directly with the business concept for current and historical patient demographic and administrative information. The presence of MRN, name, DOB, gender, and emergency contact fields makes this a patient master/demographics record rather than a clinical encounter or insurance table.

per_question.question: What claims have been submitted for a specific patient's treatments?
per_question.answer:
I cannot identify the actual claims for a specific patient from the retrieved context, because the knowledge graph here contains schema definitions and business glossary content, not operational patient or claim records.

From the schema, the relevant structure is this: `TREATMENTS` stores the patient’s treatments and procedures, including `treatment_id`, `patient_id`, `diagnosis_id`, `treatment_name`, `cpt_code`, `provider_id`, `department_id`, `treatment_date`, and `treatment_status`. `CLAIMS` stores insurance claims and billing information for services rendered, including `claim_id`, `patient_id`, `insurance_plan_id`, `claim_number`, `service_date`, `submission_date`, `cpt_code`, `icd_10_code`, and, from the concept mapping, also `amount_charged`, `amount_allowed`, `amount_paid`, `claim_status`, `denial_reason`, `is_deleted`, `valid_from`, `valid_to`, `created_at`, and `updated_at`. `PATIENTS` provides the patient identifier via `patient_id` and `mrn`. `DIAGNOSES` stores `diagnosis_id`, `patient_id`, and `icd_10_code`.

At the schema level, to trace claims submitted for a specific patient’s treatments, you would identify the patient in `PATIENTS` using `mrn` or `patient_id`, join to `TREATMENTS` on `PATIENTS.patient_id = TREATMENTS.patient_id`, and join to `CLAIMS` on `PATIENTS.patient_id = CLAIMS.patient_id`. Because the retrieved context does not define a direct foreign key from `CLAIMS` to `TREATMENTS`, the strongest matching fields available in the schema are the shared `patient_id` and `cpt_code`; you can also align `TREATMENTS.treatment_date` with `CLAIMS.service_date`. If you want diagnosis support for the treatment-to-claim match, join `TREATMENTS.diagnosis_id = DIAGNOSES.diagnosis_id` and compare `DIAGNOSES.icd_10_code` to `CLAIMS.icd_10_code`.

So, the schema supports finding submitted claims for a patient’s treatments by combining patient, procedure, date, and diagnosis information, but the context does not contain the actual claim rows needed to answer which claims were submitted for any specific patient.

## Financial & Administrative **Insurance** A third-party payer contract that provides coverage for medical services. Insurance types include private commercial plans, Medicare (federal), Medicaid (state), and TRICARE (military). Each insurance plan has specific coverage policies, prior authorization requirements, and cost-sharing structures. **Prior Authorization** The process of obtaining advance approval from an insurance payer before certain services, treatments, or medications can be provided. Authorization requirements vary by payer, procedure, and medical necessity criteria. **Claim** A formal request for payment submitted to an insurance payer for services rendered to an insured patient. Claims include procedure codes (CPT/HCPCS), diagnosis codes (ICD-10), modifiers, and charges. Claims may be submitted on a fee-for-service or bundled payment basis. **Explanation of Benefits (EOB)** A document from the insurance payer explaining what services were covered, what portion was paid to the provider, and what financial responsibility remains for the patient (deductibles, copayments, coinsurance). **Coordination of Benefits (COB)** The process of determining which insurance plan pays first when a patient is covered by multiple payers. Rules are governed by state law and contract terms. ## Scheduling & Access **Appointment** A scheduled encounter between a patient and a provider for clinical services. Appointments can be in-person office visits, telehealth consultations, or procedural sessions. Status types include scheduled, confirmed, checked-in, in-progress, completed, canceled, and no-show. **Appointment Type** The classification of an appointment based on purpose, duration, and resource requirements. Types include new patient evaluation, established patient follow-up, annual wellness visit, procedure, and imaging. **Patient Access** The functional area responsible for scheduling, registration, insurance verification, pre-certification, and financial counseling. Patient Access departments are often the first point of contact for patients entering the healthcare system. ## Clinical Workflows **Clinical Documentation** The creation and maintenance of electronic health record entries reflecting patient care, clinical decision-making, and treatment plans. Documentation must meet regulatory standards for completeness, accuracy, and timeliness. **Care Coordination** The deliberate organization of patient care activities between multiple participants involved in a patient's care to facilitate appropriate delivery of healthcare services. Coordination includes transitions of care, specialist referrals, and post-acute planning. **Transitions of Care** The movement of patients between healthcare settings, locations, or levels of care. Critical transition points include admission, transfer between units, discharge to post-acute facilities, and home health referral. ## Data Quality & Integrity **Data Governance** The policies, processes, and standards ensuring high-quality data throughout its lifecycle. Governance includes data stewardship, quality metrics, privacy protocols, and audit trails. **Historization** The practice of maintaining time-bounded versions of data records to capture state changes over time. Effective dating (valid_from, valid_to) allows reconstruction of historical states for auditing and clinical review. **Soft Delete** A logical deletion mechanism where records are marked as inactive rather than physically removed. Soft deletes preserve audit trails and allow recovery of accidentally deleted records. ## Regulatory & Privacy **HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act)** Federal legislation regulating the use and disclosure of Protected Health Information (PHI). HIPAA establishes national standards for privacy, security, and breach notification. **Protected Health Information (PHI)** Individually identifiable health information transmitted or maintained in electronic, paper, or oral forms. PHI includes demographic data, medical histories, test results, and insurance information. **Minimum Necessary Standard** The HIPAA principle that only the minimum amount of PHI necessary to accomplish the intended purpose should be used, disclosed, or requested. **Patient Privacy Rights** Under HIPAA, patients have rights to access their medical records, request amendments, obtain an accounting of disclosures, and request restrictions on certain uses and disclosures of their information.

per_question.question: Which providers in a specific department have ordered abnormal lab results?
per_question.answer:
The knowledge graph does not contain actual provider or lab result records, so it cannot tell you which specific providers in a department have ordered abnormal lab results. It contains schema metadata only.

From the schema, the relevant way to answer this is to use LAB_RESULTS and PROVIDERS. LAB_RESULTS stores the lab result and includes ordering_provider_id, patient_id, test_name, loinc_code, test_value, unit, reference_range, is_abnormal, result_date, notes, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, and updated_at. PROVIDERS stores provider details and includes provider_id, npi, first_name, last_name, provider_type, specialty, department_id, and is_active.

The join path is LAB_RESULTS.ordering_provider_id = PROVIDERS.provider_id. To restrict to a specific department, filter on PROVIDERS.department_id = the target department. To restrict to abnormal results, filter on LAB_RESULTS.is_abnormal = TRUE.

So the provider-identifying fields available from the schema are in PROVIDERS: provider_id, npi, first_name, last_name, provider_type, specialty, and department_id. The abnormal lab evidence comes from LAB_RESULTS.is_abnormal, along with test_name, loinc_code, test_value, unit, reference_range, result_date, and notes if you want the supporting result details.

A schema-level query pattern would therefore be: find providers from PROVIDERS whose provider_id matches LAB_RESULTS.ordering_provider_id, where PROVIDERS.department_id is the department of interest and LAB_RESULTS.is_abnormal is true. If you only want active providers, the schema also provides PROVIDERS.is_active. LAB_RESULTS also has is_deleted and historization fields valid_from and valid_to, but the context does not define an exact rule for selecting only current rows.

## Financial & Administrative **Insurance** A third-party payer contract that provides coverage for medical services. Insurance types include private commercial plans, Medicare (federal), Medicaid (state), and TRICARE (military). Each insurance plan has specific coverage policies, prior authorization requirements, and cost-sharing structures. **Prior Authorization** The process of obtaining advance approval from an insurance payer before certain services, treatments, or medications can be provided. Authorization requirements vary by payer, procedure, and medical necessity criteria. **Claim** A formal request for payment submitted to an insurance payer for services rendered to an insured patient. Claims include procedure codes (CPT/HCPCS), diagnosis codes (ICD-10), modifiers, and charges. Claims may be submitted on a fee-for-service or bundled payment basis. **Explanation of Benefits (EOB)** A document from the insurance payer explaining what services were covered, what portion was paid to the provider, and what financial responsibility remains for the patient (deductibles, copayments, coinsurance). **Coordination of Benefits (COB)** The process of determining which insurance plan pays first when a patient is covered by multiple payers. Rules are governed by state law and contract terms. ## Scheduling & Access **Appointment** A scheduled encounter between a patient and a provider for clinical services. Appointments can be in-person office visits, telehealth consultations, or procedural sessions. Status types include scheduled, confirmed, checked-in, in-progress, completed, canceled, and no-show. **Appointment Type** The classification of an appointment based on purpose, duration, and resource requirements. Types include new patient evaluation, established patient follow-up, annual wellness visit, procedure, and imaging. **Patient Access** The functional area responsible for scheduling, registration, insurance verification, pre-certification, and financial counseling. Patient Access departments are often the first point of contact for patients entering the healthcare system. ## Clinical Workflows **Clinical Documentation** The creation and maintenance of electronic health record entries reflecting patient care, clinical decision-making, and treatment plans. Documentation must meet regulatory standards for completeness, accuracy, and timeliness. **Care Coordination** The deliberate organization of patient care activities between multiple participants involved in a patient's care to facilitate appropriate delivery of healthcare services. Coordination includes transitions of care, specialist referrals, and post-acute planning. **Transitions of Care** The movement of patients between healthcare settings, locations, or levels of care. Critical transition points include admission, transfer between units, discharge to post-acute facilities, and home health referral. ## Data Quality & Integrity **Data Governance** The policies, processes, and standards ensuring high-quality data throughout its lifecycle. Governance includes data stewardship, quality metrics, privacy protocols, and audit trails. **Historization** The practice of maintaining time-bounded versions of data records to capture state changes over time. Effective dating (valid_from, valid_to) allows reconstruction of historical states for auditing and clinical review. **Soft Delete** A logical deletion mechanism where records are marked as inactive rather than physically removed. Soft deletes preserve audit trails and allow recovery of accidentally deleted records. ## Regulatory & Privacy **HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act)** Federal legislation regulating the use and disclosure of Protected Health Information (PHI). HIPAA establishes national standards for privacy, security, and breach notification. **Protected Health Information (PHI)** Individually identifiable health information transmitted or maintained in electronic, paper, or oral forms. PHI includes demographic data, medical histories, test results, and insurance information. **Minimum Necessary Standard** The HIPAA principle that only the minimum amount of PHI necessary to accomplish the intended purpose should be used, disclosed, or requested. **Patient Privacy Rights** Under HIPAA, patients have rights to access their medical records, request amendments, obtain an accounting of disclosures, and request restrictions on certain uses and disclosures of their information.

## Billing & Revenue Cycle **Charge Capture** The process of recording services and procedures for billing purposes. Charges must be captured at the time of service and linked to appropriate billing codes. **Claim Scrubbing** Automated validation of claims against payer-specific rules, coding guidelines, and regulatory requirements before submission. Scrubbing reduces claim rejections and denials. **Denial Management** The systematic process of tracking, analyzing, and appealing claim denials from payers. Effective denial management identifies root causes and implements corrective actions. **Revenue Cycle Management** The administrative and clinical functions that contribute to the capture, management, and collection of patient service revenue. The cycle spans from patient scheduling through final payment collection. ## Quality & Performance **Clinical Quality Measures** Standardized metrics assessing the quality of care delivered across specific conditions and procedures. Examples include readmission rates, mortality rates, complication rates, and adherence to evidence-based guidelines. **Value-Based Care** Payment models that reward healthcare providers for delivering high-quality, cost-efficient care rather than volume of services. Models include Accountable Care Organizations (ACOs) and bundled payments. **Population Health** The health outcomes of a group of individuals, including the distribution of such outcomes within the group. Population health management focuses on improving outcomes for defined patient panels through preventive care and chronic disease management.

per_question.question: What is a patient's complete care journey including diagnoses, treatments, and medications?
per_question.answer:
The knowledge graph contains schema-level metadata and business definitions, not actual patient-level records, so I cannot list a specific patient’s complete care journey. What it does show is how a patient’s care journey is represented in the database and how to reconstruct it.

A patient’s journey starts with the Patient concept and the PATIENTS table, which stores the patient master/demographic record. PATIENTS is the anchor table for the journey and includes patient_id, MRN, first_name, last_name, date_of_birth, gender, phone, email, emergency contact information, primary_insurance_id, and historization fields such as valid_from, valid_to, plus is_deleted for soft delete handling. The glossary also states that a patient may have multiple episodes of care across different time periods.

Diagnoses are represented in the DIAGNOSES table, which stores patient diagnosis records with ICD-10 coding and temporal tracking. To attach diagnoses to a patient, join DIAGNOSES.patient_id to PATIENTS.patient_id. The diagnosis record includes diagnosis_id, patient_id, icd_10_code, diagnosis_name, diagnosis_type, provider_id, and diagnosis_date. The business glossary adds the clinical meaning of diagnosis types: a principal diagnosis is the condition chiefly responsible for admission, while comorbidity is an additional concurrent diagnosis. The DIAGNOSES table’s diagnosis_type values shown are principal, comorbidity, admitting, and secondary.

Treatments are represented directly by the TREATMENTS table, which implements the Treatments business concept. To attach treatments to a patient, join TREATMENTS.patient_id to PATIENTS.patient_id. To connect a treatment to its clinical justification, join TREATMENTS.diagnosis_id to DIAGNOSES.diagnosis_id. This is important because the glossary states that each treatment is linked to specific diagnoses through clinical justification. The TREATMENTS table includes treatment_id, patient_id, diagnosis_id, treatment_name, cpt_code, provider_id, department_id, treatment_date, treatment_status, notes, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, and updated_at. This means the treatment portion of the journey can show what intervention was performed, when it occurred, which diagnosis it was for, which provider performed it, and in which department it occurred. The CPT/HCPCS procedure code also supports billing traceability.

Medications are represented in the MEDICATIONS table. To attach medications to a patient, join MEDICATIONS.patient_id to PATIENTS.patient_id. The visible medication attributes include medication_id, patient_id, medication_name, ndc_code, dosage, route, and frequency. The glossary further states that each medication record includes dosage, route of administration, frequency, and prescribing provider. The visible route values are oral, IV, IM, topical, inhaled, and subcutaneous. So the medication portion of the journey describes what pharmaceutical therapy the patient received and how it was administered.

If you want the most complete schema-level care journey, the join path is PATIENTS.patient_id = DIAGNOSES.patient_id, PATIENTS.patient_id = TREATMENTS.patient_id, PATIENTS.patient_id = MEDICATIONS.patient_id, and TREATMENTS.diagnosis_id = DIAGNOSES.diagnosis_id. You can also enrich diagnoses and treatments with provider details by joining DIAGNOSES.provider_id = PROVIDERS.provider_id and TREATMENTS.provider_id = PROVIDERS.provider_id, since PROVIDERS stores provider identity, NPI, provider_type, specialty, and department affiliation. Treatments can also be tied to organizational context through TREATMENTS.department_id = DEPARTMENTS.department_id, although the DEPARTMENTS table definition is not included in the retrieved context.

So, from the available context, a patient’s complete care journey is modeled as: the patient master record in PATIENTS; one or more diagnosis records in DIAGNOSES with ICD-10 coding, dates, and diagnosis classification; one or more treatment records in TREATMENTS linked both to the patient and, when applicable, to a specific diagnosis; and one or more medication records in MEDICATIONS linked to the patient with dosage, route, and frequency details. Because PATIENTS and TREATMENTS explicitly include valid_from, valid_to, and is_deleted, and the glossary defines historization and soft delete, the model also supports reconstructing the journey over time rather than only showing the current state.

# Business Glossary - Advanced Healthcare Management System ## Patient Management **Patient** An individual receiving medical care, treatment, or services from the healthcare organization. Each patient has a unique medical record number and may have multiple episodes of care across different time periods. Patients can be identified by their medical record number (MRN), which is distinct from any external insurance identifiers. **Patient Registration** The process of capturing essential demographic and administrative information when a patient first presents to the healthcare system. This includes personal details, emergency contacts, insurance information, and consent forms. ## Clinical Concepts **Diagnosis** The identification of a disease, condition, or injury based on clinical evaluation, laboratory results, and diagnostic imaging. Diagnoses are coded using ICD-10-CM (International Classification of Diseases, 10th Revision, Clinical Modification) for billing and epidemiological purposes. A patient may have multiple concurrent diagnoses with varying levels of clinical priority. **Principal Diagnosis** The primary condition identified after study as chiefly responsible for admission of the patient to the healthcare facility. This diagnosis determines the primary diagnosis-related group (DRG) for billing purposes. **Comorbidity** Any additional diagnosis that exists simultaneously with the principal diagnosis. Comorbidities affect patient complexity scores and reimbursement levels under risk-adjusted payment models. **Treatment** Any medical intervention, therapeutic procedure, or clinical action taken to address a diagnosed condition. Treatments range from medication administration to surgical procedures and behavioral therapies. Each treatment is linked to specific diagnoses through clinical justification. **Medication** Any pharmaceutical substance administered to a patient for therapeutic purposes. Medications include prescription drugs, over-the-counter medications, and controlled substances. Each medication record includes dosage, route of administration, frequency, and prescribing provider. **Medication Reconciliation** The process of comparing a patient's medication orders to all medications the patient has been taking to avoid errors, omissions, duplications, and drug interactions. **LabResult** The outcome of diagnostic laboratory tests performed on patient specimens (blood, urine, tissue, etc.). Results include quantitative values, reference ranges, abnormality flags, and interpreting pathologist notes. Results are timestamped and linked to specific test orders. ## Provider & Organizational Structure **Provider** A licensed healthcare professional authorized to deliver medical services within their scope of practice. Provider types include physicians (MD/DO), nurse practitioners (NP), physician assistants (PA), registered nurses (RN), and clinical specialists. Each provider has unique National Provider Identifier (NPI) numbers and hospital credentialing status. **Attending Physician** The primary physician responsible for a patient's care during an admission or encounter. This physician has ultimate authority over treatment decisions and discharge planning. **Consulting Physician** A specialist called upon to provide expert opinion or manage specific aspects of a patient's condition beyond the attending physician's expertise. **Department** An organizational unit within the healthcare facility dedicated to specific medical specialties or services (e.g., Emergency Department, Cardiology, Oncology, Radiology). Departments have hierarchical relationships and may be subdivided into sub-specialties. **Service Line** A strategic grouping of related departments focused on a specific patient population or disease continuum (e.g., Heart & Vascular, Neuroscience, Orthopedics). Service lines are used for operational and financial reporting.

## Financial & Administrative **Insurance** A third-party payer contract that provides coverage for medical services. Insurance types include private commercial plans, Medicare (federal), Medicaid (state), and TRICARE (military). Each insurance plan has specific coverage policies, prior authorization requirements, and cost-sharing structures. **Prior Authorization** The process of obtaining advance approval from an insurance payer before certain services, treatments, or medications can be provided. Authorization requirements vary by payer, procedure, and medical necessity criteria. **Claim** A formal request for payment submitted to an insurance payer for services rendered to an insured patient. Claims include procedure codes (CPT/HCPCS), diagnosis codes (ICD-10), modifiers, and charges. Claims may be submitted on a fee-for-service or bundled payment basis. **Explanation of Benefits (EOB)** A document from the insurance payer explaining what services were covered, what portion was paid to the provider, and what financial responsibility remains for the patient (deductibles, copayments, coinsurance). **Coordination of Benefits (COB)** The process of determining which insurance plan pays first when a patient is covered by multiple payers. Rules are governed by state law and contract terms. ## Scheduling & Access **Appointment** A scheduled encounter between a patient and a provider for clinical services. Appointments can be in-person office visits, telehealth consultations, or procedural sessions. Status types include scheduled, confirmed, checked-in, in-progress, completed, canceled, and no-show. **Appointment Type** The classification of an appointment based on purpose, duration, and resource requirements. Types include new patient evaluation, established patient follow-up, annual wellness visit, procedure, and imaging. **Patient Access** The functional area responsible for scheduling, registration, insurance verification, pre-certification, and financial counseling. Patient Access departments are often the first point of contact for patients entering the healthcare system. ## Clinical Workflows **Clinical Documentation** The creation and maintenance of electronic health record entries reflecting patient care, clinical decision-making, and treatment plans. Documentation must meet regulatory standards for completeness, accuracy, and timeliness. **Care Coordination** The deliberate organization of patient care activities between multiple participants involved in a patient's care to facilitate appropriate delivery of healthcare services. Coordination includes transitions of care, specialist referrals, and post-acute planning. **Transitions of Care** The movement of patients between healthcare settings, locations, or levels of care. Critical transition points include admission, transfer between units, discharge to post-acute facilities, and home health referral. ## Data Quality & Integrity **Data Governance** The policies, processes, and standards ensuring high-quality data throughout its lifecycle. Governance includes data stewardship, quality metrics, privacy protocols, and audit trails. **Historization** The practice of maintaining time-bounded versions of data records to capture state changes over time. Effective dating (valid_from, valid_to) allows reconstruction of historical states for auditing and clinical review. **Soft Delete** A logical deletion mechanism where records are marked as inactive rather than physically removed. Soft deletes preserve audit trails and allow recovery of accidentally deleted records. ## Regulatory & Privacy **HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act)** Federal legislation regulating the use and disclosure of Protected Health Information (PHI). HIPAA establishes national standards for privacy, security, and breach notification. **Protected Health Information (PHI)** Individually identifiable health information transmitted or maintained in electronic, paper, or oral forms. PHI includes demographic data, medical histories, test results, and insurance information. **Minimum Necessary Standard** The HIPAA principle that only the minimum amount of PHI necessary to accomplish the intended purpose should be used, disclosed, or requested. **Patient Privacy Rights** Under HIPAA, patients have rights to access their medical records, request amendments, obtain an accounting of disclosures, and request restrictions on certain uses and disclosures of their information.

per_question.question: Which insurance plans have the highest claim denial rates?
per_question.answer:
I cannot determine which insurance plans have the highest claim denial rates from the retrieved context because it contains schema and business glossary metadata only, not actual claim records or aggregates.

From the schema, the analysis would be based on CLAIMS joined to INSURANCE_PLANS through CLAIMS.insurance_plan_id = INSURANCE_PLANS.plan_id. The relevant insurance plan attributes are in INSURANCE_PLANS: plan_name, payer_name, plan_type, prior_auth_required, and is_active. The relevant claim attributes are in CLAIMS: claim_id, insurance_plan_id, claim_number, service_date, submission_date, cpt_code, icd_10_code, amount_charged, amount_allowed, amount_paid, claim_status, and denial_reason.

A denial-rate calculation would therefore be performed at the schema level by grouping claims by insurance plan and comparing denied claims to total claims for each plan. The context indicates CLAIMS has a claim_status field and a denial_reason field, but it does not define the allowed claim_status values, so the exact logic for identifying a “denied” claim cannot be specified from the context alone. If using current active data only, the general conventions say queries should filter for valid_to IS NULL and is_deleted = FALSE. That applies to major tables and the concept-table mappings show both CLAIMS and INSURANCE_PLANS include valid_from, valid_to, and is_deleted.

So, the answerable part is: use CLAIMS.insurance_plan_id → INSURANCE_PLANS.plan_id, group by INSURANCE_PLANS.plan_name and/or payer_name, count total claims, count claims identified as denied via CLAIMS.claim_status, and compute denied ÷ total. The context does not contain the actual data needed to say which plans are highest.

contexts:
## CLAIMS Table
**Purpose**: Store insurance claims and billing information for services rendered. | Column Name | Data Type | Constraint | Description | |-------------|-----------|------------|-------------| | claim_id | INTEGER | PRIMARY KEY | Surrogate key for claim records | | patient_id | INTEGER | FK → patients.patient_id, NOT NULL | Patient associated with claim | | insurance_plan_id | INTEGER | FK → insurance_plans.plan_id, NOT NULL | Insurance plan billed | | claim_number | VARCHAR(50) | UNIQUE NOT NULL | Unique claim identifier for tracking | | service_date | DATE | NOT NULL | Date of service | | submission_date | DATE | NOT NULL | Date claim submitted to payer | | cpt_code | VARCHAR(10) | NOT NULL | Procedure code billed | | icd_10_code | VARCHAR(10) | NOT NULL | Diagnosis code supporting medical necessity

Business concept 'insurance claims and billing information for services rendered' is implemented by physical table CLAIMS (columns: claim_id, patient_id, insurance_plan_id, claim_number, service_date, submission_date, cpt_code, icd_10_code, amount_charged, amount_allowed, amount_paid, claim_status, denial_reason, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, updated_at).

## General Conventions

### Temporal Validity Pattern
All major tables use `valid_from` and `valid_to` timestamps for historization: - Current records have `valid_to = NULL` - Historical records have non-NULL `valid_to` - Queries must include `WHERE valid_to IS NULL` to retrieve current data - Historical analysis possible by querying all records ### Soft Delete Pattern All tables include `is_deleted` flag: - `is_deleted = FALSE` for active records - `is_deleted = TRUE` for logically deleted records - Queries must include `WHERE is_deleted = FALSE` to exclude deleted records - Soft deletes preserve audit trail ### Audit Timestamps All tables include: - `created_at` - set once on INSERT - `updated_at` - updated on each UPDATE ### Naming Conventions - Table names: plural_snake_case (e.g., `insurance_plans`, `lab_results`) - Column names: snake_case - Foreign keys: `{table}_{column}` (e.g., `patient_id`, `provider_id`) - In

Business concept 'Insurance Payer Plan' — The table purpose explicitly says it stores insurance payer and plan information for coverage, and the columns payer_name, plan_name, plan_type, and prior_auth_required confirm a payer-specific plan master record. This is a direct match to the corresponding business concept rather than a generic insurance plan abstraction. is implemented by physical table INSURANCE_PLANS (columns: plan_id, plan_name, payer_name, plan_type, prior_auth_required, is_active, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, updated_at).

Business concept 'Patient Demographics' — The table stores patient identity, contact, insurance linkage, and historized administrative/demographic attributes, which aligns directly with the business concept for current and historical patient demographic and administrative information. The presence of MRN, name, DOB, gender, and emergency contact fields makes this a patient master/demographics record rather than a clinical encounter or insurance table. is implemented by physical table PATIENTS (columns: patient_id, mrn, first_name, last_name, date_of_birth, gender, phone, email, emergency_contact_name, emergency_contact_phone, primary_insurance_id, is_deleted, valid_from, valid_to, created_at, updated_at).

## Financial & Administrative **Insurance** A third-party payer contract that provides coverage for medical services. Insurance types include private commercial plans, Medicare (federal), Medicaid (state), and TRICARE (military). Each insurance plan has specific coverage policies, prior authorization requirements, and cost-sharing structures. **Prior Authorization** The process of obtaining advance approval from an insurance payer before certain services, treatments, or medications can be provided. Authorization requirements vary by payer, procedure, and medical necessity criteria. **Claim** A formal request for payment submitted to an insurance payer for services rendered to an insured patient. Claims include procedure codes (CPT/HCPCS), diagnosis codes (ICD-10), modifiers, and charges. Claims may be submitted on a fee-for-service or bundled payment basis. **Explanation of Benefits (EOB)** A document from the insurance payer explaining what services were covered, what portion was paid to the provider, and what financial responsibility remains for the patient (deductibles, copayments, coinsurance). **Coordination of Benefits (COB)** The process of determining which insurance plan pays first when a patient is covered by multiple payers. Rules are governed by state law and contract terms. ## Scheduling & Access **Appointment** A scheduled encounter between a patient and a provider for clinical services. Appointments can be in-person office visits, telehealth consultations, or procedural sessions. Status types include scheduled, confirmed, checked-in, in-progress, completed, canceled, and no-show. **Appointment Type** The classification of an appointment based on purpose, duration, and resource requirements. Types include new patient evaluation, established patient follow-up, annual wellness visit, procedure, and imaging. **Patient Access** The functional area responsible for scheduling, registration, insurance verification, pre-certification, and financial counseling. Patient Access departments are often the first point of contact for patients entering the healthcare system. ## Clinical Workflows **Clinical Documentation** The creation and maintenance of electronic health record entries reflecting patient care, clinical decision-making, and treatment plans. Documentation must meet regulatory standards for completeness, accuracy, and timeliness. **Care Coordination** The deliberate organization of patient care activities between multiple participants involved in a patient's care to facilitate appropriate delivery of healthcare services. Coordination includes transitions of care, specialist referrals, and post-acute planning. **Transitions of Care** The movement of patients between healthcare settings, locations, or levels of care. Critical transition points include admission, transfer between units, discharge to post-acute facilities, and home health referral. ## Data Quality & Integrity **Data Governance** The policies, processes, and standards ensuring high-quality data throughout its lifecycle. Governance includes data stewardship, quality metrics, privacy protocols, and audit trails. **Historization** The practice of maintaining time-bounded versions of data records to capture state changes over time. Effective dating (valid_from, valid_to) allows reconstruction of historical states for auditing and clinical review. **Soft Delete** A logical deletion mechanism where records are marked as inactive rather than physically removed. Soft deletes preserve audit trails and allow recovery of accidentally deleted records. ## Regulatory & Privacy **HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act)** Federal legislation regulating the use and disclosure of Protected Health Information (PHI). HIPAA establishes national standards for privacy, security, and breach notification. **Protected Health Information (PHI)** Individually identifiable health information transmitted or maintained in electronic, paper, or oral forms. PHI includes demographic data, medical histories, test results, and insurance information. **Minimum Necessary Standard** The HIPAA principle that only the minimum amount of PHI necessary to accomplish the intended purpose should be used, disclosed, or requested. **Patient Privacy Rights** Under HIPAA, patients have rights to access their medical records, request amendments, obtain an accounting of disclosures, and request restrictions on certain uses and disclosures of their information.

## Billing & Revenue Cycle **Charge Capture** The process of recording services and procedures for billing purposes. Charges must be captured at the time of service and linked to appropriate billing codes. **Claim Scrubbing** Automated validation of claims against payer-specific rules, coding guidelines, and regulatory requirements before submission. Scrubbing reduces claim rejections and denials. **Denial Management** The systematic process of tracking, analyzing, and appealing claim denials from payers. Effective denial management identifies root causes and implements corrective actions. **Revenue Cycle Management** The administrative and clinical functions that contribute to the capture, management, and collection of patient service revenue. The cycle spans from patient scheduling through final payment collection. ## Quality & Performance **Clinical Quality Measures** Standardized metrics assessing the quality of care delivered across specific conditions and procedures. Examples include readmission rates, mortality rates, complication rates, and adherence to evidence-based guidelines. **Value-Based Care** Payment models that reward healthcare providers for delivering high-quality, cost-efficient care rather than volume of services. Models include Accountable Care Organizations (ACOs) and bundled payments. **Population Health** The health outcomes of a group of individuals, including the distribution of such outcomes within the group. Population health management focuses on improving outcomes for defined patient panels through preventive care and chronic disease management.

# Data Dictionary - Advanced Healthcare Management System

## Database Overview
**Database Name**: `advanced_healthcare_db` **Schema Version**: `3.2.1` **Environment**: Production **Last Updated**: 2025-03-15 --- ## PATIENTS Table **Purpose**: Store current and historical patient demographic and administrative information. | Column Name | Data Type | Constraint | Description | |-------------|-----------|------------|-------------| | patient_id | INTEGER | PRIMARY KEY | Surrogate key for patient records | | mrn | VARCHAR(20) | UNIQUE NOT NULL | Medical Record Number - unique patient identifier | | first_name | VARCHAR(50) | NOT NULL | Patient's legal first name | | last_name | VARCHAR(50) | NOT NULL | Patient's legal last name | | date_of_birth | DATE | NOT NULL | Patient's date of birth for age calculation | | gender | VARCHAR(10) | NOT NULL, CHECK IN ('M', 'F', 'O', 'U') | Gender: Male

# Business Glossary - Advanced Healthcare Management System ## Patient Management **Patient** An individual receiving medical care, treatment, or services from the healthcare organization. Each patient has a unique medical record number and may have multiple episodes of care across different time periods. Patients can be identified by their medical record number (MRN), which is distinct from any external insurance identifiers. **Patient Registration** The process of capturing essential demographic and administrative information when a patient first presents to the healthcare system. This includes personal details, emergency contacts, insurance information, and consent forms. ## Clinical Concepts **Diagnosis** The identification of a disease, condition, or injury based on clinical evaluation, laboratory results, and diagnostic imaging. Diagnoses are coded using ICD-10-CM (International Classification of Diseases, 10th Revision, Clinical Modification) for billing and epidemiological purposes. A patient may have multiple concurrent diagnoses with varying levels of clinical priority. **Principal Diagnosis** The primary condition identified after study as chiefly responsible for admission of the patient to the healthcare facility. This diagnosis determines the primary diagnosis-related group (DRG) for billing purposes. **Comorbidity** Any additional diagnosis that exists simultaneously with the principal diagnosis. Comorbidities affect patient complexity scores and reimbursement levels under risk-adjusted payment models. **Treatment** Any medical intervention, therapeutic procedure, or clinical action taken to address a diagnosed condition. Treatments range from medication administration to surgical procedures and behavioral therapies. Each treatment is linked to specific diagnoses through clinical justification. **Medication** Any pharmaceutical substance administered to a patient for therapeutic purposes. Medications include prescription drugs, over-the-counter medications, and controlled substances. Each medication record includes dosage, route of administration, frequency, and prescribing provider. **Medication Reconciliation** The process of comparing a patient's medication orders to all medications the patient has been taking to avoid errors, omissions, duplications, and drug interactions. **LabResult** The outcome of diagnostic laboratory tests performed on patient specimens (blood, urine, tissue, etc.). Results include quantitative values, reference ranges, abnormality flags, and interpreting pathologist notes. Results are timestamped and linked to specific test orders. ## Provider & Organizational Structure **Provider** A licensed healthcare professional authorized to deliver medical services within their scope of practice. Provider types include physicians (MD/DO), nurse practitioners (NP), physician assistants (PA), registered nurses (RN), and clinical specialists. Each provider has unique National Provider Identifier (NPI) numbers and hospital credentialing status. **Attending Physician** The primary physician responsible for a patient's care during an admission or encounter. This physician has ultimate authority over treatment decisions and discharge planning. **Consulting Physician** A specialist called upon to provide expert opinion or manage specific aspects of a patient's condition beyond the attending physician's expertise. **Department** An organizational unit within the healthcare facility dedicated to specific medical specialties or services (e.g., Emergency Department, Cardiology, Oncology, Radiology). Departments have hierarchical relationships and may be subdivided into sub-specialties. **Service Line** A strategic grouping of related departments focused on a specific patient population or disease continuum (e.g., Heart & Vascular, Neuroscience, Orthopedics). Service lines are used for operational and financial reporting.
